APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses a core NLP problem (causal/discourse relation disambiguation) using standard ML/NLP methods (LSTM RNNs, pre-trained GloVe embeddings) and compares to a clear prior state-of-the-art (Hidey and McKeown, 2016) on the AltLex corpus, so it is squarely within the scope and audience of top NLP/ML venues. The task formulation and evaluation framework (baselines B1/B2, precision/recall analysis) match venue expectations. One minor concern is that experiments appear limited to the AltLex corpus (the authors themselves note dataset scarcity), which may constrain the breadth of evaluation expected at top-tier conferences. | B(5): This is squarely an NLP problem (causality/discourse) and uses methods expected at top venues: LSTM-based encoding and pre-trained GloVe embeddings (Pennington et al., 2014) to disambiguate causal lexical markers. The paper evaluates on the AltLex corpus and compares to the state-of-the-art SVM system of Hidey and McKeown (2016), and it situates itself among relevant prior work (Bethard & Martin, Mirza & Tonelli), indicating proper audience and positioning for an ACL/EMNLP-style venue. The task formulation, experimental setup (baselines, corpora) and metrics described align with community standards for NLP/ML conferences.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a core NLP problem (causal/discourse relation disambiguation) using standard ML/NLP methods (LSTM RNNs, pre-trained GloVe embeddings) and compares to a clear prior state-of-the-art (Hidey and McKeown, 2016) on the AltLex corpus, so it is squarely within the scope and audience of top NLP/ML venues. The task formulation and evaluation framework (baselines B1/B2, precision/recall analysis) match venue expectations. One minor concern is that experiments appear limited to the AltLex corpus (the authors themselves note dataset scarcity), which may constrain the breadth of evaluation expected at top-tier conferences.

reviewer_b.justification: This is squarely an NLP problem (causality/discourse) and uses methods expected at top venues: LSTM-based encoding and pre-trained GloVe embeddings (Pennington et al., 2014) to disambiguate causal lexical markers. The paper evaluates on the AltLex corpus and compares to the state-of-the-art SVM system of Hidey and McKeown (2016), and it situates itself among relevant prior work (Bethard & Martin, Mirza & Tonelli), indicating proper audience and positioning for an ACL/EMNLP-style venue. The task formulation, experimental setup (baselines, corpora) and metrics described align with community standards for NLP/ML conferences.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper addresses an important practical problem—disambiguating causal lexical markers—and demonstrates a clear empirical improvement over the prior state-of-the-art (Hidey and McKeown 2016) on the AltLex corpus by using word embeddings and LSTM-based context encoding, with cited qualitative wins on difficult markers (e.g. “which then”, uses of “break”). By showing that contextual neural encodings improve causal vs non-causal classification without heavy reliance on lexical resources, it is likely to influence method choice in this subfield and encourage broader use of contextual representations. However, the evaluation is confined to the (limited) AltLex dataset and the contribution is primarily an application of existing neural techniques to this task, so its impact is moderate rather than transformative. | B(3): The paper tackles the important task of disambiguating causal lexical markers and demonstrates that neural context encoding (LSTM + word embeddings) outperforms the prior SVM-based state of the art on the AltLex corpus (Hidey & McKeown, 2016), including for highly ambiguous expressions (e.g. “which then”, verbs like “break”). This provides useful evidence that contextual neural representations generalize beyond resource-heavy lexical features and will likely influence practitioners working on discourse and event causality. However, impact is limited by evaluation on a single, relatively narrow corpus (the AltLex variants) and by the acknowledged scarcity of larger, representative datasets, so the work is a solid, moderate contribution rather than transformative.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem—disambiguating causal lexical markers—and demonstrates a clear empirical improvement over the prior state-of-the-art (Hidey and McKeown 2016) on the AltLex corpus by using word embeddings and LSTM-based context encoding, with cited qualitative wins on difficult markers (e.g. “which then”, uses of “break”). By showing that contextual neural encodings improve causal vs non-causal classification without heavy reliance on lexical resources, it is likely to influence method choice in this subfield and encourage broader use of contextual representations. However, the evaluation is confined to the (limited) AltLex dataset and the contribution is primarily an application of existing neural techniques to this task, so its impact is moderate rather than transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles the important task of disambiguating causal lexical markers and demonstrates that neural context encoding (LSTM + word embeddings) outperforms the prior SVM-based state of the art on the AltLex corpus (Hidey & McKeown, 2016), including for highly ambiguous expressions (e.g. “which then”, verbs like “break”). This provides useful evidence that contextual neural representations generalize beyond resource-heavy lexical features and will likely influence practitioners working on discourse and event causality. However, impact is limited by evaluation on a single, relatively narrow corpus (the AltLex variants) and by the acknowledged scarcity of larger, representative datasets, so the work is a solid, moderate contribution rather than transformative.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): The paper has a recognisable experimental flow (sections on task, data, system, experiments) but suffers from heavy repetition and fragmentary editing that breaks logical progression (e.g. the paragraph beginning “Those theories based on generative semantics…” appears verbatim many times, and passages about Bethard and Martin (2008) are duplicated). The main contribution (a neural network architecture and the claim that context encoding is required) is stated, but it is scattered and not highlighted early and clearly. Notation and formal definitions are largely absent in the excerpt (only informal mentions of LSTM, embeddings, optimizers), and figures/tables are referenced (Table 3, Table 8) but not shown here, reducing informativeness. Overall writing contains repeated sentences, awkward phrasing and some grammatical slips (e.g. “disambiguate its causal meaning”), which impede readability. | B(3): The paper has a recognizable structure (sections and subsections such as 3, 4, 4.1, 4.3) and the main contribution is stated (a neural network architecture for causality disambiguation), but the flow is disrupted by extensive repetition and duplicated paragraphs (e.g. the generative semantics and Bethard & Martin passages appear many times). Notation is minimal so not problematic, but figures/tables are only referenced (Table 3, Table 8) and not present in the excerpt, and examples are poorly formatted; this harms clarity. Writing contains grammatical errors and awkward phrasing (e.g. “lacks of representative”, “correctly disambiguate”) and LaTeX/artifact remnants, indicating the manuscript needs careful editing for readability.

The main contribution (a neural network architecture and the claim that context encoding is required) is stated, but it is scattered and not highlighted early and clearly. Notation and formal definitions are largely absent in the excerpt (only informal mentions of LSTM, embeddings, optimizers), and figures/tables are referenced (Table 3, Table 8) but not shown here, reducing informativeness. Overall writing contains repeated sentences, awkward phrasing and some grammatical slips (e.g. “disambiguate its causal meaning”), which impede readability.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper has a recognizable structure (sections and subsections such as 3, 4, 4.1, 4.3) and the main contribution is stated (a neural network architecture for causality disambiguation), but the flow is disrupted by extensive repetition and duplicated paragraphs (e.g. the generative semantics and Bethard & Martin passages appear many times). Notation is minimal so not problematic, but figures/tables are only referenced (Table 3, Table 8) and not present in the excerpt, and examples are poorly formatted; this harms clarity. Writing contains grammatical errors and awkward phrasing (e.g. “lacks of representative”, “correctly disambiguate”) and LaTeX/artifact remnants, indicating the manuscript needs careful editing for readability.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper applies standard neural techniques (LSTM/context encoding with pre-trained word embeddings) to the task of disambiguating causal lexical markers and evaluates on the AltLex corpus, which previously had only an SVM+lexical-resources approach (Hidey & McKeown 2016). The authors clearly differentiate from prior work by avoiding heavy lexical resources and claiming better generalization and improved results, but the method is primarily an application of established neural architectures to a specific dataset rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic idea. This yields a moderate (incremental) novelty: useful and empirically relevant, but not a non-trivial methodological advance that would surprise experts. | B(3): The paper applies standard neural techniques (LSTM RNNs with pretrained GloVe embeddings) to disambiguate causal lexical markers and evaluates on the AltLex corpus (c.f. text mentioning LSTM, GloVe and AltLex). It clearly differentiates itself from prior work (e.g. Hidey & McKeown 2016’s SVM with lexical-resource features) by avoiding heavy feature engineering and reporting improved results. However, the core method is an application of existing neural models to this task/dataset rather than a fundamentally new algorithm or theoretical perspective, so the novelty is moderate/mostly empirical.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies standard neural techniques (LSTM/context encoding with pre-trained word embeddings) to the task of disambiguating causal lexical markers and evaluates on the AltLex corpus, which previously had only an SVM+lexical-resources approach (Hidey & McKeown 2016). The authors clearly differentiate from prior work by avoiding heavy lexical resources and claiming better generalization and improved results, but the method is primarily an application of established neural architectures to a specific dataset rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic idea. This yields a moderate (incremental) novelty: useful and empirically relevant, but not a non-trivial methodological advance that would surprise experts.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies standard neural techniques (LSTM RNNs with pretrained GloVe embeddings) to disambiguate causal lexical markers and evaluates on the AltLex corpus (c.f. text mentioning LSTM, GloVe and AltLex). It clearly differentiates itself from prior work (e.g. Hidey & McKeown 2016’s SVM with lexical-resource features) by avoiding heavy feature engineering and reporting improved results. However, the core method is an application of existing neural models to this task/dataset rather than a fundamentally new algorithm or theoretical perspective, so the novelty is moderate/mostly empirical.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against sensible baselines: a majority-class baseline (B1) and the prior state-of-the-art on the same AltLex corpus (Hidey and McKeown, 2016) (sections 4 and 4.1), and uses both corpus variants. Comparisons appear fair (same data, same corpus versions) and include qualitative analysis showing examples where the neural model outperforms B2, and discussion of why feature-based SVMs are limited. However, the evaluation is narrowly focused on a single prior system (no other recent neural or broader baselines, limited reporting of metrics/statistical tests), so while comparisons are good they omit some potentially relevant alternatives. | B(4): The paper compares directly to the clear state-of-the-art on the same dataset (Hidey and McKeown 2016 is used as baseline B2) and includes a sensible simple baseline (B1); it also evaluates on both AltLex corpus versions (noted in Section 4). The authors discuss differences in methodology (SVM with lexical resources vs neural embeddings and contextual encoding) and cite and discuss other relevant prior work (Bethard & Martin, Mirza & Tonelli, Riaz & Girju, Do et al.). However, the excerpt lacks explicit detail that comparisons used identical training/test splits, metrics and experimental conditions, and it does not acknowledge cases where prior methods might perform comparably or better, so the comparison is good but with minor omissions in fairness/transparency.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against sensible baselines: a majority-class baseline (B1) and the prior state-of-the-art on the same AltLex corpus (Hidey and McKeown, 2016) (sections 4 and 4.1), and uses both corpus variants. Comparisons appear fair (same data, same corpus versions) and include qualitative analysis showing examples where the neural model outperforms B2, and discussion of why feature-based SVMs are limited. However, the evaluation is narrowly focused on a single prior system (no other recent neural or broader baselines, limited reporting of metrics/statistical tests), so while comparisons are good they omit some potentially relevant alternatives.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares directly to the clear state-of-the-art on the same dataset (Hidey and McKeown 2016 is used as baseline B2) and includes a sensible simple baseline (B1); it also evaluates on both AltLex corpus versions (noted in Section 4). The authors discuss differences in methodology (SVM with lexical resources vs neural embeddings and contextual encoding) and cite and discuss other relevant prior work (Bethard & Martin, Mirza & Tonelli, Riaz & Girju, Do et al.). However, the excerpt lacks explicit detail that comparisons used identical training/test splits, metrics and experimental conditions, and it does not acknowledge cases where prior methods might perform comparably or better, so the comparison is good but with minor omissions in fairness/transparency.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper states clear claims (Sections 3–4) that a neural LSTM-based model using pre-trained GloVe embeddings outperforms baselines (B1 majority-class and B2 Hidey & McKeown SVM) on the AltLex corpus and that context encoding is necessary. The experimental setup includes reasonable baselines and both versions of the AltLex corpus (mentions of Table 3, Table 8), but the excerpt provides no quantitative results, no statistical significance testing, and limited details on splits, hyperparameters or class imbalance handling, so the performance/ generalization claims are not fully verifiable. There are also unsupported broad claims of better generalization from embeddings despite evaluation on a single corpus, which is a logical limitation. Mathematical content is minimal and non-problematic. | B(3): The paper states a clear main claim—that a neural LSTM using word embeddings can disambiguate causal lexical markers and outperforms the Hidey & McKeown (2016) SVM baseline—and presents experiments on the AltLex corpus (see Sections 4, 4.1 and references to Table 8). Mathematical content is minimal/none (no derivations), and the methodology uses reasonable baselines (B1 majority-class and B2 the prior SVM) and standard tools (GloVe embeddings, LSTM, Adam/Adadelta). However, the evaluation is limited to the AltLex corpus (acknowledged in Section 4), quantitative results and significance testing are not shown in the provided excerpt, and the generalization claim (that embeddings give higher generalization than lexical resources) is asserted but only demonstrated on a single dataset, leaving concerns about robustness and statistical support.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear claims (Sections 3–4) that a neural LSTM-based model using pre-trained GloVe embeddings outperforms baselines (B1 majority-class and B2 Hidey & McKeown SVM) on the AltLex corpus and that context encoding is necessary. The experimental setup includes reasonable baselines and both versions of the AltLex corpus (mentions of Table 3, Table 8), but the excerpt provides no quantitative results, no statistical significance testing, and limited details on splits, hyperparameters or class imbalance handling, so the performance/ generalization claims are not fully verifiable. There are also unsupported broad claims of better generalization from embeddings despite evaluation on a single corpus, which is a logical limitation. Mathematical content is minimal and non-problematic.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states a clear main claim—that a neural LSTM using word embeddings can disambiguate causal lexical markers and outperforms the Hidey & McKeown (2016) SVM baseline—and presents experiments on the AltLex corpus (see Sections 4, 4.1 and references to Table 8). Mathematical content is minimal/none (no derivations), and the methodology uses reasonable baselines (B1 majority-class and B2 the prior SVM) and standard tools (GloVe embeddings, LSTM, Adam/Adadelta). However, the evaluation is limited to the AltLex corpus (acknowledged in Section 4), quantitative results and significance testing are not shown in the provided excerpt, and the generalization claim (that embeddings give higher generalization than lexical resources) is asserted but only demonstrated on a single dataset, leaving concerns about robustness and statistical support.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The experiments are adequate but limited: evaluation is only on the AltLex corpus (they do use both non‑bootstrapped and bootstrapped versions) and compare against a majority baseline and the Hidey & McKeown SVM system, supporting the main claim but lacking broader corpora or statistical testing. Ablation/sensitivity analysis is minimal (they report testing two optimizers and report a "best configuration") but there is no systematic ablation of model components or hyperparameter sensitivity. Related work is discussed extensively and fairly (Bethard & Martin, Mirza & Tonelli, Hidey & McKeown, Riaz & Girju, etc.). Some failure cases and dataset/lexical‑resource limitations are mentioned (examples for ‘break’ and ‘which then’ and corpus coverage), but deeper error analysis and broader limitations are not provided. Reproducibility is partially supported (GloVe 840B 300d, LSTM, optimizers noted) but many implementation and training details (architecture specifics, hyperparameters, splits, statistical significance, code) are missing. | B(3): Experiments compare against reasonable baselines (B1 and the SVM of Hidey and McKeown) and use both AltLex corpus versions (section 4), but evaluation is limited to that single dataset so evidence is narrow. No clear ablation of model components is reported (they only mention testing two optimizers and using GloVe/LSTM but do not show ablations of context encoding vs. alternatives). Related work is extensively discussed and cited throughout the text, and some failure analysis is given via qualitative examples and discussion of ambiguous markers (Table 8). Reproducibility is partial: embeddings and optimizers are specified but architectural/hyperparameter and training details necessary to reproduce results are not provided.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are adequate but limited: evaluation is only on the AltLex corpus (they do use both non‑bootstrapped and bootstrapped versions) and compare against a majority baseline and the Hidey & McKeown SVM system, supporting the main claim but lacking broader corpora or statistical testing. Ablation/sensitivity analysis is minimal (they report testing two optimizers and report a "best configuration") but there is no systematic ablation of model components or hyperparameter sensitivity. Related work is discussed extensively and fairly (Bethard & Martin, Mirza & Tonelli, Hidey & McKeown, Riaz & Girju, etc.). Some failure cases and dataset/lexical‑resource limitations are mentioned (examples for ‘break’ and ‘which then’ and corpus coverage), but deeper error analysis and broader limitations are not provided. Reproducibility is partially supported (GloVe 840B 300d, LSTM, optimizers noted) but many implementation and training details (architecture specifics, hyperparameters, splits, statistical significance, code) are missing.

reviewer_b.justification: Experiments compare against reasonable baselines (B1 and the SVM of Hidey and McKeown) and use both AltLex corpus versions (section 4), but evaluation is limited to that single dataset so evidence is narrow. No clear ablation of model components is reported (they only mention testing two optimizers and using GloVe/LSTM but do not show ablations of context encoding vs. alternatives). Related work is extensively discussed and cited throughout the text, and some failure analysis is given via qualitative examples and discussion of ambiguous markers (Table 8). Reproducibility is partial: embeddings and optimizers are specified but architectural/hyperparameter and training details necessary to reproduce results are not provided.
